questions.question: What is a continuation bet (c-bet)?
options: A bet on the flop made by the player who was the preflop aggressor
Any bet made by the player in the big blind
A mandatory bet required before the flop is dealt
A bet that can only be made on the river

explanations.A: Correct. The preflop raiser 'continues' their aggression by betting the flop, leveraging initiative and range advantage.
explanations.B: The c-bet is defined by preflop initiative, not by which blind a player posted.
explanations.C: Nothing forces a flop bet; a c-bet is a voluntary continuation of preflop aggression.
explanations.D: A c-bet is specifically a flop bet by the preflop raiser, not a river action.

questions.question: The main strategic edge that makes c-betting profitable is:
options: The fact that c-bets are free and cost no chips
A combination of range advantage and fold equity from preflop initiative
A rule that opponents must fold to any flop bet
The guarantee that the c-bettor holds the best hand

explanations.A: C-bets risk chips like any other bet; the edge comes from range advantage and fold equity.
explanations.B: Correct. The raiser usually has the stronger range and opponents often missed the flop, so betting wins folds or builds value.
explanations.C: Opponents are never forced to fold; c-bets work because many hands genuinely miss the flop.
explanations.D: C-bets are frequently bluffs; the bettor does not need the best hand to profit.

questions.question: Which scenario is NOT a continuation bet?
options: The preflop raiser bets a dry ace-high flop after a single caller
The preflop raiser bets a wet flop for protection
The big blind, who only called preflop, leads out betting the flop
The preflop 3-bettor bets the flop in a 3-bet pot

explanations.A: This is a textbook c-bet: the preflop raiser bets the flop.
explanations.B: This is still a c-bet; the raiser is continuing on the flop.
explanations.C: Correct. A lead by the preflop caller is a donk bet, not a c-bet, because that player was not the preflop aggressor.
explanations.D: The 3-bettor is the preflop aggressor, so a flop bet here is a c-bet.

questions.question: A 'missed c-bet' refers to:
options: The preflop raiser betting too large on the flop
A bet that fails to make the opponent fold
Folding the flop after betting it
The preflop raiser checking the flop instead of betting

explanations.A: Sizing too large is not a 'missed' c-bet; the bet still happened.
explanations.B: A c-bet that gets called still occurred; 'missed' refers to not betting at all.
explanations.C: You cannot fold a bet you just made on the same street; 'missed' means the bet was skipped.
explanations.D: Correct. When the aggressor declines to bet the flop, that spot is described as a checked-back or missed c-bet.

questions.question: In a single-raised pot heads-up, who typically holds preflop initiative on the flop?
options: The player who made the last raise before the flop
The player who posted the small blind
Whichever player acts first on the flop
The player with the larger remaining stack

explanations.A: Correct. Initiative belongs to the last preflop aggressor, who is then positioned to c-bet.
explanations.B: Posting a blind is a forced action, not the initiative that comes from raising.
explanations.C: Acting order depends on position, not on who holds preflop initiative.
explanations.D: Stack size does not determine who took the aggressive preflop line.

questions.question: A 'dry' flop is best described as:
options: A board with three cards of the same suit
An uncoordinated board with few draws that connects with few hands
A board containing two connected high cards
Any flop that contains an ace

explanations.A: Three of a suit is a monotone, very wet texture, not a dry one.
explanations.B: Correct. Dry boards like K-7-2 rainbow offer almost no draws and miss most calling ranges.
explanations.C: Connected high cards create straight potential, which makes a board wetter, not dry.
explanations.D: An ace alone does not make a board dry; texture depends on connectivity and suits.

questions.question: On a dry flop like K♠ 7♥ 2♦, the preflop raiser usually prefers to:
options: Bet very large to deny equity
Check the entire range
C-bet small at a high frequency
Only bet with complete air

explanations.A: There is little draw equity to deny on a dry board, so large sizing is rarely needed.
explanations.B: Checking everything surrenders the range and initiative advantage the dry board provides.
explanations.C: Correct. Few draws exist and the raiser's range hits the king often, so cheap, frequent c-bets are efficient.
explanations.D: Betting only air is unbalanced and exploitable; the raiser bets a mix of value and bluffs.

questions.question: Why is a small c-bet sizing effective on dry boards?
options: It guarantees the opponent will always call
It is the only legal bet size on the flop
It builds a huge pot to maximize variance
It risks few chips while still folding out the many hands that completely missed

explanations.A: Small sizing tends to fold out air, not guarantee calls; it is cheap fold equity.
explanations.B: Players may choose any legal size; small sizing is a strategic choice, not a rule.
explanations.C: Small bets keep the pot modest; building a huge pot is the opposite effect.
explanations.D: Correct. On dry textures there is little draw equity to protect, so a small bet captures fold equity cheaply.

questions.question: A rainbow flop means:
options: The three flop cards are all different suits, so no flush draw is possible yet
All three cards are the same suit
Two of the three cards share a suit
The board is paired

explanations.A: Correct. With three distinct suits, no two cards share a suit, so a one-card flush draw cannot exist on the flop.
explanations.B: Three of one suit is monotone, the opposite of rainbow.
explanations.C: Two of a suit creates a flush draw; rainbow means all three suits differ.
explanations.D: A pair describes ranks, not suits; rainbow refers to having three different suits.

questions.question: Against a capped big-blind range on K♠ 8♣ 3♦, the raiser benefits because:
options: The caller is guaranteed to hold top pair
The raiser's range contains more kings and strong pairs than the caller's
The board gives the caller many flush draws
Position is irrelevant on dry boards

explanations.A: The capped caller misses this board far more often than they hit a king.
explanations.B: Correct. The preflop raiser holds more big broadway and pair combos, giving a clear range and nut advantage on this dry king-high board.
explanations.C: A rainbow K-8-3 offers no flush draws at all.
explanations.D: Position still matters; the point here is the range advantage, not its absence.

questions.question: A 'wet' flop is characterized by:
options: Three completely unconnected low cards of different suits
The total absence of any possible draws
High connectivity with many straight and flush draws available
Any board where the high card is an ace

explanations.A: That description fits a dry board, not a wet one.
explanations.B: A wet board is defined by the abundance of draws, not their absence.
explanations.C: Correct. Coordinated boards like J♠ T♠ 9♣ connect with many hands and produce numerous draws.
explanations.D: An ace-high board can be dry; wetness depends on connectivity and suits, not the top card.

questions.question: On a wet flop such as 9♥ 8♥ 7♣, the preflop raiser often shifts toward:
options: A tiny bet with the entire range
Never betting under any circumstance
Betting only the weakest hands in the range
A lower c-bet frequency, using larger sizing with strong hands and draws

explanations.A: Tiny sizing fails to charge the many draws a wet board creates.
explanations.B: Strong hands and good draws still want to bet for value and protection.
explanations.C: Betting only air is unbalanced; value hands and draws are the core of a wet-board betting range.
explanations.D: Correct. Because the caller's range connects well, the raiser bets more selectively and larger to charge draws.

questions.question: Why does larger c-bet sizing make sense on wet boards?
options: It charges opponents' many draws a higher price and builds the pot with value hands
It is the only way to bluff on any board
It removes the opponent's ability to ever fold
It prevents the turn card from being dealt

explanations.A: Correct. With heavy draw density, bigger bets deny equity and grow the pot when you are ahead.
explanations.B: Bluffs can use many sizes; large sizing on wet boards is about charging draws.
explanations.C: Larger bets generally increase folds from weak hands, not eliminate folding.
explanations.D: Bet sizing has no effect on whether future cards appear.

questions.question: Which flop is the wettest?
options: Q♦ J♦ T♣

explanations.A: Ace-high rainbow with gaps is a classic dry board.
explanations.B: Correct. This board offers an open-ended straight structure plus a diamond flush draw, the most connected of the options.
explanations.C: King-high rainbow with no connectivity is dry.
explanations.D: Queen-high rainbow with large gaps offers very few draws.

questions.question: On a monotone flop (three of one suit), a key adjustment is:
options: Treating it exactly like a dry rainbow board
Assuming no one can ever have a flush
Recognizing that flushes and flush draws are already live, often favoring controlled sizing
Always shoving all-in regardless of holding

explanations.A: A monotone board is wet by nature and must not be played like a dry rainbow.
explanations.B: A made flush is possible the moment three suited cards appear.
explanations.C: Correct. Monotone boards mean a made flush or one-card flush draw is immediately possible, so ranges and sizing shift accordingly.
explanations.D: Blind shoving ignores the nuanced range interactions a monotone board creates.

questions.question: The goal of a value bet is to:
options: Make every better hand fold immediately
Reach showdown without putting in chips
Force the pot to be split evenly
Get called by hands that are worse than yours

explanations.A: Folding out better hands is the goal of a bluff, not a value bet.
explanations.B: A value bet by definition wagers chips; checking is what avoids betting.
explanations.C: A split requires tied hands and is not the purpose of value betting.
explanations.D: Correct. Value betting extracts chips from second-best hands that are willing to call.

questions.question: A 'thin' value bet refers to:
options: Betting a hand that is only slightly ahead of the hands likely to call
Betting the absolute nuts for maximum size
Betting purely as a bluff with no equity
Checking a strong hand to induce bluffs

explanations.A: Correct. Thin value targets marginal calls where you are a small favorite against the calling range.
explanations.B: Betting the nuts is value, but it is not thin; thin value involves marginal edges.
explanations.C: A value bet, even a thin one, expects to be ahead of calls; that is not a bluff.
explanations.D: Checking to induce is not a bet at all, let alone a thin value bet.

questions.question: When choosing value-bet sizing on a dry board against a capped range, a common choice is:
options: The maximum overbet, to fold out all worse hands
A smaller bet, because worse hands are more willing to call a cheaper price
A check, since value hands should never bet
A bet only on the river and never earlier

explanations.A: Overbetting often folds out the very hands you want to value bet against on a dry board.
explanations.B: Correct. On dry boards a small size keeps weak made hands and bluff-catchers in, maximizing called value.
explanations.C: Value hands bet to extract chips; checking forfeits that value.
explanations.D: Value can and should be taken across multiple streets, not just the river.

questions.question: Top pair top kicker on K♠ 7♦ 2♣ against a single caller should usually:
options: Check and give up, treating it as a weak hand
Only bluff with it because it cannot win at showdown
Bet for value, since many worse pairs and draws will call
Fold to any aggression immediately

explanations.A: Top pair top kicker is strong and should be betting for value, not surrendering.
explanations.B: It is a clear made hand with strong showdown value, not a bluff.
explanations.C: Correct. This is a strong made hand on a dry board; betting extracts value from worse kings, second pairs, and floats.
explanations.D: Folding the near-top of your range to a single caller is far too weak.

questions.question: Choosing a larger value size on the river is most justified when:
options: Your opponent is known to fold everything but the nuts
You are bluffing and want folds
The pot is already empty with no chips in play
Your opponent's range is wide and contains many strong second-best hands that can call big

explanations.A: Against a nit who only calls with the nuts, large value sizing gets no worse hands to call.
explanations.B: Sizing discussion here is about value; getting folds is the bluff objective.
explanations.C: There is always a pot to contest postflop; an empty pot is not a real scenario here.
explanations.D: Correct. A wide, call-happy range with strong-but-beaten hands lets you size up and still get paid.

questions.question: A bluff bet succeeds primarily by:
options: Generating fold equity so better hands give up the pot
Getting called by worse hands for value
Always producing the best hand at showdown
Guaranteeing a split pot

explanations.A: Correct. A bluff profits when better hands fold, since it cannot win at showdown.
explanations.B: Being called by worse is the value-bet outcome, not the bluff's goal.
explanations.C: A bluff usually holds a weak hand; its profit comes from folds, not showdown strength.
explanations.D: Bluffs aim to win the whole pot via folds, not to force a tie.

questions.question: The best bluffing candidates on the flop often have:
options: Strong made hands with no need to fold anyone out
Some backdoor equity or blockers, so they can improve or reduce villain's strong combos
Exactly the nuts every single time
No relationship to the board or villain's range

explanations.A: Strong made hands prefer to value bet; they are not ideal pure bluffs.
explanations.B: Correct. Bluffs with backdoor draws or key blockers retain equity and unblock folds, improving their EV.
explanations.C: The nuts is a value hand, the opposite of a bluffing candidate.
explanations.D: Good bluffs are chosen precisely for their equity and blocker interaction with the board and range.

questions.question: A bluff c-bet on a dry board works best when:
options: The opponent almost always flopped a strong pair
You already hold the nuts
The opponent's range largely misses and folds at a high rate
There are no chips in the pot to win

explanations.A: If villain frequently has a strong pair, fold equity collapses and the bluff loses value.
explanations.B: Holding the nuts means you should value bet, not bluff.
explanations.C: Correct. Dry boards leave callers with many unpaired hands that fold to pressure.
explanations.D: A bluff needs a pot worth taking; an empty pot makes the bluff pointless.

questions.question: Bluffing too frequently relative to your value bets makes you:
options: Unbeatable, since opponents can never adjust
Guaranteed to win every pot
Immune to pot odds considerations
Exploitable, because an observant opponent can profitably call you down

explanations.A: Opponents readily adjust by calling lighter when you bluff too much.
explanations.B: Excessive bluffing leads to getting caught and losing pots, not winning them all.
explanations.C: Pot odds always apply and in fact reward opponents who call your over-bluffs.
explanations.D: Correct. An over-bluffed range lets opponents widen their calls and capture EV against you.

questions.question: A 'give-up' decision means:
options: Choosing to check or fold a bluff because fold equity has become too low to continue
Betting larger to force the issue regardless of equity
Always calling down with a busted hand
Showing your cards to the table

explanations.A: Correct. When barreling further is unlikely to fold out better hands, abandoning the bluff is correct.
explanations.B: Betting bigger with no fold equity only burns chips; giving up means stopping.
explanations.C: Calling with a busted bluff has no showdown value and is not 'giving up'.
explanations.D: Giving up refers to ending the bluff, not exposing your hand.

questions.question: All else equal, a larger bet size relative to the pot tends to:
options: Encourage more calls from weak hands
Apply more pressure and fold out more of the opponent's weak hands
Have no effect on the opponent's pot odds
Make the hand end immediately in a chop

explanations.A: Larger bets generally fold out weak hands rather than attracting them.
explanations.B: Correct. Bigger bets give worse pot odds to call, increasing folds from marginal hands.
explanations.C: Bet size directly changes the price and therefore the opponent's pot odds.
explanations.D: Sizing does not force a chop; it influences fold and call decisions.

questions.question: A polarized betting range (only strong value and bluffs) generally prefers:
options: The smallest legal bet size every time
Only checking, never betting
A larger bet size, often up to or above pot
A size that exactly matches the opponent's stack name

explanations.A: Tiny sizing underuses the leverage that a polarized range is built to exploit.
explanations.B: A polarized range is by definition a betting range, not a checking one.
explanations.C: Correct. Polarized ranges leverage big sizing to maximize value and apply maximum bluff pressure.
explanations.D: Sizing is chosen from range structure and pot, not from any 'stack name'.

questions.question: A merged or 'medium-strength' value range typically uses:
options: A massive overbet to fold everyone out
No bet at all under any circumstance
A size based solely on the dealer button color
A smaller bet size to get called by the many worse hands it beats

explanations.A: Overbetting folds out the worse hands a merged range wants to get called by.
explanations.B: Merged value hands still bet; they just choose smaller sizing.
explanations.C: Sizing derives from range and pot dynamics, not arbitrary table features.
explanations.D: Correct. Merged ranges value-bet thinner hands, so a smaller size keeps weaker calls in.

questions.question: If the pot is $100 and you bet $50, your opponent is being offered pot odds of:
options: Needing about 25% equity to call ($50 to win $200)
Needing about 50% equity to call
Needing about 10% equity to call
No equity at all, since calls are always free

explanations.A: Correct. They call $50 into a $150 pot, risking 50 to win 200 total, which is 50/200 = 25%.
explanations.B: Half-pot bets require only ~25% equity, not 50%.
explanations.C: 10% understates it; the correct break-even for a half-pot bet is ~25%.
explanations.D: Calls cost chips; the opponent must have sufficient equity to justify the call.

questions.question: A pot-sized bet ($100 into $100) gives the caller a break-even equity of about:
options: 33%, since they call $100 to win $300 total

explanations.A: 25% corresponds to a half-pot bet, not a full pot-sized bet.
explanations.B: Correct. Calling 100 into a 200 pot risks 100 to win 300, so 100/300 ≈ 33%.
explanations.C: 50% would require a 2x-pot overbet, not a pot-sized bet.
explanations.D: 66% is far too high; a pot bet needs only ~33% equity to call.

questions.question: An overbet of twice the pot ($200 into $100) requires the caller to have at least:
options: About 25% equity
About 10% equity
About 40% equity ($200 to win $500 total)
About 66% equity

explanations.A: 25% applies to a half-pot bet; a 2x overbet demands far more.
explanations.B: 10% drastically understates the equity needed against a 2x-pot overbet.
explanations.C: Correct. They risk 200 to win 500, so 200/500 = 40% break-even equity.
explanations.D: 66% overstates it; the correct break-even for a 2x-pot bet is 40%.

questions.question: Pot odds tell you:
options: The exact two cards your opponent is holding
How many players remain in the tournament
The rake the house will collect
The minimum equity your hand needs to make calling a bet break even

explanations.A: Pot odds are a price calculation, not a read on specific hole cards.
explanations.B: Pot odds describe the current bet-to-pot price, unrelated to field size.
explanations.C: Rake is a separate fee; pot odds concern the equity needed to call.
explanations.D: Correct. Pot odds convert the price of a call into the equity threshold required to continue profitably.

questions.question: Facing a $25 bet into a $75 pot, you must call $25 to win:
options: $100, needing 25/125 = 20% equity to break even
$25, needing 50% equity
$75, needing 33% equity
$200, needing 12.5% equity

explanations.A: Correct. The pot becomes $100 after the bet, you call $25, so 25/125 = 20% break-even.
explanations.B: You win the whole pot plus the bet, $100, not just your own $25.
explanations.C: The total you can win is the $75 pot plus the $25 bet = $100.
explanations.D: The math overstates the pot; the relevant figure is $100 to win, giving 20%.

questions.question: A flush draw with 9 outs on the flop has roughly what chance to complete by the river?
options: About 19%
About 35%
About 50%
About 8%

explanations.A: 19% is the chance with one card to come, not from flop through river.
explanations.B: Correct. Nine outs over two cards is approximately 35% (the 'rule of 4' gives 9×4 ≈ 36%).
explanations.C: 9 outs do not reach a coinflip; ~35% is the flop-to-river figure.
explanations.D: 8% would be a gutshot for one card, far below a flush draw's two-card equity.

questions.question: An open-ended straight draw (8 outs) has about what equity from flop to river?
options: About 17%
About 45%
About 32%
About 4%

explanations.A: 17% is the one-card equity for an open-ender, not the full flop-to-river figure.
explanations.B: 8 outs fall short of 45%; the two-card equity is about 32%.
explanations.C: Correct. Eight outs over two cards is roughly 8×4 = 32% by the rule of 4.
explanations.D: 4% is far too low; that is closer to a single overcard-pair shot, not an open-ender.

questions.question: A gutshot straight draw (4 outs) hitting on the next single card is approximately:
options: About 17%
About 35%
About 50%
About 8.5%

explanations.A: 17% is closer to an open-ended draw's one-card chance, not a 4-out gutshot.
explanations.B: 35% is a flush draw's two-card equity, far above a single-card gutshot.
explanations.C: A 4-out gutshot is nowhere near a coinflip on one card.
explanations.D: Correct. Four outs for one card is roughly 4×2 = 8% by the rule of 2.

questions.question: If a bet gives you 25% pot odds but your draw only has 18% equity to hit, you should generally:
options: Fold, unless implied odds or fold equity change the math
Always call, since any draw is worth chasing
Always raise, because raising fixes bad odds
Ignore the numbers and go by feel

explanations.A: Correct. Raw equity below the required 25% means a pure call is unprofitable absent extra value.
explanations.B: Chasing draws below the required equity loses chips over time.
explanations.C: Raising risks more chips with insufficient equity unless it carries fold equity.
explanations.D: Disregarding pot odds undermines disciplined, profitable decisions.

questions.question: Implied odds refer to:
options: The chips already in the pot only
The extra chips you expect to win on later streets if your draw completes
The rake taken once the hand ends
The number of players who have folded

explanations.A: Those are direct pot odds; implied odds look ahead to future betting.
explanations.B: Correct. Implied odds account for future value beyond the immediate pot, which can justify a call with raw equity below the bet's price.
explanations.C: Rake is unrelated to the future winnings implied odds describe.
explanations.D: Implied odds concern future chip gains, not the count of folded players.

questions.question: Equity realization describes:
options: The exact percentage of pots the dealer wins
The number of chips in the starting stack
How much of a hand's raw equity you actually capture by showdown given position and playability
A rule that all hands realize 100% of their equity

explanations.A: The dealer does not contest pots; realization is about your captured equity.
explanations.B: Stack size is not what equity realization measures.
explanations.C: Correct. Raw equity is theoretical; realization measures the share you actually win after betting and position effects.
explanations.D: Many hands realize less than their raw equity, especially out of position.

questions.question: Being in position improves equity realization mainly because:
options: It forces opponents to show their cards early
It increases the size of every pot automatically
It guarantees you the best hand at showdown
You act last, gaining information and controlling the pot size more effectively

explanations.A: Position does not reveal hole cards; its value is informational and pot-control based.
explanations.B: Position helps control pot size; it does not inflate every pot.
explanations.C: Position improves decisions but never guarantees the winning hand.
explanations.D: Correct. Acting last lets you check behind, value bet precisely, and realize more of your equity.

questions.question: A hand that is hard to play out of position, like a weak suited connector, tends to:
options: Realize less than its raw equity
Realize more than 100% of its equity always
Have zero equity in every pot
Be unaffected by position

explanations.A: Correct. Out-of-position playability problems mean such hands capture less of their theoretical equity.
explanations.B: No hand reliably exceeds its raw equity; tough OOP hands under-realize.
explanations.C: Suited connectors have meaningful equity; the issue is realizing it OOP.
explanations.D: Position strongly affects how well such marginal hands realize equity.

questions.question: Checking back a marginal made hand in position can improve realization by:
options: Forcing the opponent to fold the best hand
Reaching a cheaper showdown and avoiding being raised off the hand
Guaranteeing you win the pot at showdown
Adding extra chips to the pot for value

explanations.A: Checking does not fold out a better hand; it simply controls the pot.
explanations.B: Correct. Controlling the pot lets a marginal hand realize its showdown equity without overcommitting.
explanations.C: Checking back controls risk but does not guarantee a win.
explanations.D: Checking adds no chips; it is the pot-control alternative to betting.

questions.question: Denying equity refers to:
options: Letting opponents see every card for free
Refusing to ever bet your strong hands
Betting to make hands with outs fold before they can improve
A rule preventing draws from existing

explanations.A: Giving free cards lets opponents realize equity, the opposite of denying it.
explanations.B: Strong hands deny equity by betting, not by always checking.
explanations.C: Correct. By charging draws or folding out overcards, you prevent opponents from realizing their equity.
explanations.D: Draws still exist; denying equity is about charging or folding them out via betting.

questions.question: Range advantage on a given flop means:
options: One player happens to hold the single best hand right now
Both players have exactly equal equity
The player with more chips automatically has it
One player's overall range has more equity across the whole board than the other's

explanations.A: That is a specific holding (nut advantage at the top), not whole-range advantage.
explanations.B: Equal equity is the absence of a range advantage.
explanations.C: Range advantage is about hand-equity distribution, not stack size.
explanations.D: Correct. Range advantage compares aggregate equity of entire ranges, guiding who can bet aggressively.

questions.question: Nut advantage (as distinct from range advantage) refers to:
options: Holding a larger share of the strongest possible hands on a board
Having the higher overall range equity but no strong hands
Posting the big blind
Always having the worst hands in the deck

explanations.A: Correct. Nut advantage concerns the top of the range, which supports large and overbet sizings.
explanations.B: That describes a range edge without a nut edge; nut advantage is specifically about the strongest combos.
explanations.C: Posting blinds is unrelated to who holds the nut combos.
explanations.D: Holding the worst hands is the opposite of a nut advantage.

questions.question: On A♠ K♦ 4♣, the preflop raiser typically has a range advantage because:
options: The caller is guaranteed two pair
Their range contains more AK, AQ, AA, and KK combinations than the caller's
The board provides a flush draw to the caller
Range advantage cannot exist on ace-high boards

explanations.A: The caller rarely holds two pair here; the raiser's broadway-heavy range dominates.
explanations.B: Correct. High broadway boards favor the raiser, whose range is dense in big aces and kings.
explanations.C: A♠ K♦ 4♣ is rainbow with no flush draw.
explanations.D: Ace-high broadway boards are precisely where raisers often hold a strong range advantage.

questions.question: On a low, connected board like 6♥ 5♥ 4♣, the big-blind caller's range may be relatively strong because:
options: The raiser always has the nuts on low boards
The caller folded before the flop
The caller defends many small and connected hands that hit this texture
Range advantage is fixed and never changes by board

explanations.A: The raiser's high-card-heavy range often misses low connected boards.
explanations.B: A caller who reached the flop is contesting it, not folded.
explanations.C: Correct. Low coordinated boards connect with the caller's suited connectors and small pairs, narrowing the raiser's edge.
explanations.D: Range advantage shifts dramatically with board texture, including toward the caller here.

questions.question: When you have both range and nut advantage, you can often:
options: Only check, since strong ranges should never bet
Bet only the minimum to invite calls
Fold the majority of your hands
Bet large or overbet at a high frequency to maximize pressure

explanations.A: Strong, nutted ranges are exactly the ones that bet large, not check.
explanations.B: Min-betting underuses the leverage a nut-advantaged range possesses.
explanations.C: Folding a dominant range away forfeits its built-in edge.
explanations.D: Correct. Holding more of the strongest hands plus higher overall equity supports aggressive, large sizing.

questions.question: A polarized range consists of:
options: Strong value hands and bluffs, with few medium-strength hands
Only medium-strength hands and nothing else
Exclusively the single best hand possible
A random selection unrelated to hand strength

explanations.A: Correct. Polarized ranges split into clear value and bluff categories, which favors larger sizing.
explanations.B: That is a condensed or merged range, the opposite of polarized.
explanations.C: One hand is not a range; polarization includes both value and bluffs.
explanations.D: Polarization is a deliberate split by strength, not randomness.

questions.question: A condensed (capped) range is one that:
options: Is full of nutted hands and pure bluffs
Contains mostly medium-strength hands and lacks the very top of the range
Always includes every possible strong hand
Cannot ever call a bet

explanations.A: That describes a polarized range, not a capped one.
explanations.B: Correct. Capped ranges miss the strongest combos, making them vulnerable to large bets and overbets.
explanations.C: A capped range specifically lacks the strongest hands.
explanations.D: Capped ranges can and do call; they are simply missing top-tier holdings.

questions.question: Why does a polarized range justify larger bet sizing?
options: Because medium hands need a small bet for protection
Because large bets are required by the rules
Big bets maximize value from the strong hands and apply maximum pressure with the bluffs
Because it guarantees opponents always fold

explanations.A: A polarized range has few medium hands, so protection sizing is not the driver.
explanations.B: Sizing is a strategic choice, not a rule imposed on polarized ranges.
explanations.C: Correct. Since the range has no medium hands needing protection, large sizing leverages the value-and-bluff split.
explanations.D: Large polarized bets get called by value-catchers too; they do not guarantee folds.

questions.question: An opponent whose range is capped on the river is especially vulnerable to:
options: A tiny blocker bet that they always fold to
Checking the river every time
A min-raise that offers great odds
An overbet, since they hold no hands strong enough to comfortably call big

explanations.A: A capped range can often call small bets; the real pressure comes from large sizing.
explanations.B: Checking lets a capped range realize its showdown equity for free.
explanations.C: Min-raises give the capped range cheap continues, reducing the pressure.
explanations.D: Correct. Capped ranges lack the nutted hands needed to call large sizing, so overbets pressure them effectively.

questions.question: Putting medium-strength hands into a checking range rather than betting them helps because:
options: It protects the checking range and lets those hands realize showdown value
It maximizes value, since medium hands fold out worse
It guarantees they win at showdown
It is required because medium hands cannot legally bet

explanations.A: Correct. Checking medium hands keeps the check range from being too weak and captures cheap showdowns.
explanations.B: Medium hands betting often fold out worse and get called by better, so checking is usually better.
explanations.C: Checking controls the pot but does not guarantee winning.
explanations.D: Any hand may bet; checking medium hands is a strategic choice.

questions.question: A blocker is a card in your hand that:
options: Physically prevents the dealer from dealing
Reduces the number of strong combinations your opponent can hold
Guarantees you the best hand at showdown
Adds extra chips to your stack

explanations.A: Blockers are an informational concept, not a physical obstruction.
explanations.B: Correct. Holding a key card removes combos from villain's range, informing bluffs and calls.
explanations.C: A blocker shifts combinatorics; it does not guarantee a winning hand.
explanations.D: Blockers affect range reading, not your chip count.

questions.question: On a board where a flush is possible, holding the ace of the flush suit is valuable as a bluff because:
options: It makes your own flush unbeatable
It forces the villain to call
It blocks the nut flush, so villain is less likely to hold it and more likely to fold
It changes the board cards

explanations.A: Holding one suited ace without four flush cards does not make a flush; the value is the blocker effect.
explanations.B: Blocking the nuts encourages folds, not calls.
explanations.C: Correct. The nut-flush blocker removes villain's strongest calling combos, boosting bluff fold equity.
explanations.D: Your hole cards do not alter the community board.

questions.question: When deciding to bluff-raise, holding a blocker to the nuts is good because it:
options: Increases the chance the opponent flopped a set
Forces the dealer to reshuffle
Makes your bluff a value bet automatically
Lowers the chance the opponent has a hand strong enough to continue

explanations.A: A blocker reduces, not increases, the combos villain can have.
explanations.B: Blockers are about combinatorics, not dealer actions.
explanations.C: A blocker improves a bluff's fold equity; it does not convert a weak hand into value.
explanations.D: Correct. Removing combos of the nuts from villain's range increases the odds they fold to your raise.

questions.question: For a thin river value bet, you ideally want to:
options: Block the hands that would raise or beat you while unblocking the worse hands that call
Block all the worse hands so they cannot call
Hold cards that have no bearing on villain's range
Always hold the exact nuts

explanations.A: Correct. Good value blockers remove strong combos that beat you and keep the worse calling combos live.
explanations.B: Blocking the worse hands reduces your called value, the opposite of the goal.
explanations.C: Irrelevant cards provide no blocker benefit for thin value.
explanations.D: Thin value is by definition not the nuts; blocker selection still matters.

questions.question: Holding the K♠ on a three-spade board when bluffing is useful because:
options: It means you already have a made flush
It blocks the second-nut flush and represents the spade you could be holding
It prevents villain from ever folding
It has no effect because spades are irrelevant

explanations.A: A single spade is not a flush; its value is as a blocker and representation.
explanations.B: Correct. The K♠ removes a strong flush combo from villain and supports your story of holding spades.
explanations.C: Blocking strong flushes increases folds, not the reverse.
explanations.D: On a three-spade board, holding a high spade is a highly relevant blocker.

questions.question: A semi-bluff is a bet made with:
options: The nuts, intending to get called
A hand with literally no way to improve
A drawing hand that can win by folds now or by improving later
A made full house wanting protection

explanations.A: Betting the nuts is value, not a semi-bluff.
explanations.B: A pure bluff with no outs is not a semi-bluff; semi-bluffs have draw equity.
explanations.C: Correct. Semi-bluffs combine fold equity with the backup of completing a draw.
explanations.D: A full house is a value hand, not a drawing semi-bluff.

questions.question: Semi-bluffing a flush draw is often better than checking because:
options: A flush draw is already the best hand
Checking is against the rules with a draw
It guarantees the flush will complete
You can win immediately via folds and still have outs when called

explanations.A: A flush draw is usually behind made hands; its strength is its potential, not current value.
explanations.B: Checking a draw is perfectly legal; betting is a strategic choice.
explanations.C: Semi-bluffing does not change the odds of completing the draw.
explanations.D: Correct. The dual way to win, fold equity plus draw equity, makes semi-bluffing high-EV.

questions.question: The ideal semi-bluff candidate among these is:
options: An open-ended straight flush draw with two ways to improve to the nuts
A pair of deuces with no draw on a dangerous board
Ace-high with no draw and no backdoor
A made straight that is already the nuts

explanations.A: Correct. Maximum outs and nut potential give strong equity to back up the fold-equity play.
explanations.B: Bottom pair with no draw lacks the equity that defines a good semi-bluff.
explanations.C: Pure ace-high has minimal equity and is closer to a stone bluff than a semi-bluff.
explanations.D: The nuts is a value hand, not a semi-bluff.

questions.question: Why are semi-bluffs valuable for balancing a betting range?
options: They remove the need to ever value bet
They let you bet draws that improve to value, disguising your made hands
They make your range entirely face-up
They only work when you hold the nuts

explanations.A: Semi-bluffs complement value bets; they do not replace them.
explanations.B: Correct. Mixing draws into your betting range balances value and keeps opponents from reading your strength.
explanations.C: Mixing draws actually conceals your range rather than exposing it.
explanations.D: Semi-bluffs by definition do not hold the nuts; they hold draws.

questions.question: A semi-bluff loses much of its value when:
options: You have many outs and deep stacks
The opponent folds frequently to bets
Stacks are so shallow that there is little to win if the draw hits
Your draw is to the nuts

explanations.A: Many outs and deep stacks strengthen, not weaken, a semi-bluff.
explanations.B: High fold frequency increases a semi-bluff's value.
explanations.C: Correct. With low implied odds and thin fold equity, the draw's backup payoff shrinks, weakening the semi-bluff.
explanations.D: Drawing to the nuts improves a semi-bluff's expected value.

questions.question: A check-raise is:
options: Betting first and then folding to a raise
Calling a bet and then betting the next street
A forced action whenever you are out of position
Checking first, then raising after an opponent bets

explanations.A: That sequence is a bet-fold, not a check-raise.
explanations.B: That is a call followed by a lead, not a check-raise.
explanations.C: Check-raising is optional and strategic, never forced by position.
explanations.D: Correct. The player checks to induce a bet, then raises, often for value or as a strong bluff.

questions.question: A common reason to check-raise as the out-of-position player is to:
options: Build the pot with strong hands and add pressure with selected bluffs
Give the opponent a free card with a strong hand
Guarantee the opponent folds every time
Avoid putting any chips in the pot

explanations.A: Correct. Check-raising lets the OOP player seize aggression, growing the pot with value and applying fold equity with bluffs.
explanations.B: Check-raising charges the opponent rather than offering a free card.
explanations.C: Opponents continue with strong holdings; check-raises do not guarantee folds.
explanations.D: A check-raise adds chips; it is an aggressive action.

questions.question: Good check-raise bluff candidates often have:
options: Made full houses with no need to fold anyone
Draws or blockers that give equity and reduce villain's strong continues
No equity and no blocker relevance
The exact nuts every time

explanations.A: Full houses prefer to extract value, not to bluff-raise.
explanations.B: Correct. Equity and blockers make check-raise bluffs profitable by combining outs with fold equity.
explanations.C: Stone-cold air with no blockers makes for a poor check-raise bluff.
explanations.D: The nuts is a value check-raise, not a bluff.

questions.question: Check-raising for value works best when:
options: Your opponent never bets the flop
You hold the weakest hand in your range
Your opponent c-bets at a high frequency, so they often have something to commit
You are guaranteed to be drawing dead

explanations.A: If the opponent never bets, there is nothing to check-raise.
explanations.B: Value check-raising requires a strong hand, not the weakest one.
explanations.C: Correct. A high c-bet frequency means the opponent regularly bets hands that will pay off your check-raise.
explanations.D: Drawing dead is the worst possible situation to commit chips, not a value spot.

questions.question: On a wet board, check-raising a strong made hand is often preferred to calling because:
options: It lets all the draws see the turn for free
It signals weakness to fold out worse hands
It is the only legal response to a bet
It charges the many draws and avoids giving cheap cards

explanations.A: Calling, not raising, gives cheaper cards; raising charges the draws.
explanations.B: A check-raise represents strength and is not designed to fold out worse hands for free.
explanations.C: Calling and folding are also legal; check-raising is a strategic choice.
explanations.D: Correct. Raising denies equity to draws and builds the pot while you are ahead on a coordinated board.

questions.question: A delayed continuation bet is:
options: Checking the flop as the preflop raiser, then betting the turn
Betting the flop and then checking the turn
Raising preflop a second time
A bet that must always be pot-sized

explanations.A: Correct. The aggressor declines the flop bet and instead bets the turn, often with showdown value or improved equity.
explanations.B: That is a flop c-bet followed by a check, not a delayed c-bet.
explanations.C: A delayed c-bet is a postflop concept, unrelated to a second preflop raise.
explanations.D: Delayed c-bets can use any size; the defining trait is the flop check then turn bet.

questions.question: A delayed c-bet is often useful when:
options: The opponent has already folded the hand
You checked back the flop and the turn improves your hand or your perceived range
You want to give away your exact hand
You are forced to bet the turn by the rules

explanations.A: If the opponent folded, there is no one left to bet into.
explanations.B: Correct. Delaying lets you bet a turn that favors your story or after your hand picks up equity.
explanations.C: A delayed c-bet conceals rather than reveals your holding.
explanations.D: Betting the turn is optional; the delayed line is a strategic choice.

questions.question: One benefit of checking back the flop and delaying the c-bet is:
options: It guarantees you win the pot at showdown
It forces the opponent to fold the flop
It can induce bluffs and lets you bet a turn where the opponent's range is weaker
It always wins more than betting the flop

explanations.A: Delaying controls the line but does not guarantee a win.
explanations.B: Checking back does not fold anyone out; it passes the action.
explanations.C: Correct. Checking back caps your appearance of strength, inducing action and setting up a profitable turn bet.
explanations.D: Delaying is situational; it is not universally superior to a flop c-bet.

questions.question: Checking back a medium hand on the flop and then betting a blank turn is often:
options: Always a mistake because you must c-bet every flop
Only correct with the absolute nuts
A sign you should have folded preflop
A reasonable thin value or protection line against capped ranges

explanations.A: C-betting every flop is unbalanced; checking back has clear merit.
explanations.B: Delayed lines are frequently taken with medium hands, not just the nuts.
explanations.C: Reaching the turn with a medium hand is normal; it does not imply a preflop error.
explanations.D: Correct. The turn bet extracts value from worse hands while the flop check controlled the pot.

questions.question: Delaying the c-bet is especially attractive on flops where:
options: Your range is not strong enough to bet at a high frequency profitably
You hold a massive range and nut advantage
The opponent has already shown their cards
You want to inflate the pot as fast as possible

explanations.A: Correct. When the flop favors the caller, checking back and reassessing on the turn protects your range.
explanations.B: With a big range advantage, an immediate c-bet is usually preferred over delaying.
explanations.C: Opponents do not reveal hole cards midhand; this is not a real condition.
explanations.D: Delaying controls the pot; it is not the fastest way to inflate it.

questions.question: A probe bet is:
options: A bet by the preflop raiser on the flop
A bet by the out-of-position player on the turn after the preflop raiser checked back the flop
A mandatory minimum bet on every street
A preflop limp followed by a call

explanations.A: That is a standard c-bet, not a probe bet.
explanations.B: Correct. When the aggressor passes on the flop, the OOP player can lead the turn to probe at the now-capped range.
explanations.C: Probe bets are optional and situational, not mandatory.
explanations.D: A probe bet is a postflop turn lead, unrelated to limping.

questions.question: Probe betting is profitable largely because:
options: The opponent is guaranteed to hold the nuts
It forces a chop pot
The preflop raiser's flop check often caps their range, leaving them weak on the turn
It is required after every checked flop

explanations.A: A capped, checked-back range rarely holds the nuts, which is why probing works.
explanations.B: Probe bets aim to win the pot, not to split it.
explanations.C: Correct. A checked-back flop signals a capped range, so leading the turn applies pressure to a weak holding.
explanations.D: Probing is a strategic option, not a requirement.

questions.question: Good probe-bet candidates on the turn include:
options: Only the absolute nuts and nothing else
Exclusively hands that are drawing dead
Hands you have already folded
Hands with some equity or blockers plus weak made hands that benefit from folds

explanations.A: Probing uses a balanced range, not solely the nuts.
explanations.B: Drawing dead hands without fold equity are poor probe candidates.
explanations.C: Folded hands cannot bet; probing requires live cards.
explanations.D: Correct. Probing works with a mix of value, equity, and blocker hands against a capped opponent.

questions.question: When the flop goes check-check and you are out of position on the turn, leading out:
options: Targets the opponent's capped range and denies a free showdown to medium hands
Should never be done since you must always check to the raiser
Guarantees the opponent folds the best hand
Is only legal with a flush

explanations.A: Correct. The opponent's flop check suggests weakness, so a turn probe pressures their capped holdings.
explanations.B: Continuing to check to a raiser who already declined to bet wastes the chance to attack a capped range.
explanations.C: Probing applies pressure but does not guarantee folding out the best hand.
explanations.D: Leading the turn is legal with any holding; it is a strategic choice.

questions.question: A donk bet is:
options: A c-bet by the preflop raiser
A flop lead by the player who is not the preflop aggressor, into that aggressor
A check made out of position
A preflop raise from the button

explanations.A: A c-bet is the aggressor betting; a donk bet comes from the non-aggressor.
explanations.B: Correct. Donking means betting into the preflop raiser instead of checking to them.
explanations.C: Donk betting is a bet, not a check.
explanations.D: Donk betting is a postflop lead, not a preflop raise.

questions.question: Donk betting is most defensible when:
options: The flop is ace-high and favors the raiser
You always want to surrender initiative
The flop strongly favors the caller's range more than the preflop raiser's
You hold the worst possible hand with no plan

explanations.A: Ace-high boards favor the raiser, making a donk lead generally weak.
explanations.B: Donking is an attempt to seize initiative, not surrender it.
explanations.C: Correct. On boards that hit the caller's range hard, leading out can be justified to seize value and initiative.
explanations.D: Leading with pure air and no plan into the aggressor is typically a losing play.

questions.question: A common downside of frequent, unbalanced donk betting is that it:
options: Guarantees a profit on every flop
Forces the raiser to fold every time
Prevents the turn card from appearing
Tends to be exploitable because it often represents a narrow, readable range

explanations.A: Unbalanced donking is exploitable and does not guarantee profit.
explanations.B: The raiser can raise or call; donking does not force folds.
explanations.C: Bet choices have no effect on whether the turn is dealt.
explanations.D: Correct. Poorly constructed donk leads can be face-up, letting observant opponents respond optimally.

questions.question: On a low connected board like 7♥ 6♥ 5♣ where you are the big-blind caller, a small donk lead can:
options: Make sense, since this texture favors your range and pressures the raiser's overcards
Never be correct because donk bets are always wrong
Only be done with the stone-cold nuts
Be required by the rules of the game

explanations.A: Correct. Low coordinated boards hit the BB defending range well, so a lead can be a sound, range-based play.
explanations.B: Donk betting is situational; on caller-favoring boards it can be a valid strategy.
explanations.C: Donk leads on favorable textures use a range, not just the nuts.
explanations.D: There is no rule forcing a donk lead; it is a strategic choice.

questions.question: A double-barrel is:
options: Betting only the flop
Betting both the flop and the turn
Raising preflop twice
Checking the flop and the turn

explanations.A: A single flop bet is a single barrel, not a double-barrel.
explanations.B: Correct. Firing a second bet on the turn after a flop c-bet is a double-barrel.
explanations.C: Barreling is a postflop concept across streets, not a preflop action.
explanations.D: Two checks are not barrels; barreling means betting.

questions.question: A triple-barrel is:
options: Three preflop raises in a row
Betting only on the river
Betting the flop, turn, and river
Checking down all three streets

explanations.A: Multiple preflop raises are 3-bets and 4-bets, not barrels.
explanations.B: A single river bet is one barrel, not three.
explanations.C: Correct. Three consecutive bets across all three postflop streets form a triple-barrel.
explanations.D: Checking is not barreling; barreling requires bets.

questions.question: A good turn card to fire a second barrel as a bluff is one that:
options: Obviously improves only the caller's range
Leaves the board completely unchanged
Has already been folded by the opponent
Favors your perceived range and lets you credibly represent a strong hand

explanations.A: A card that helps the caller reduces fold equity, making the barrel worse.
explanations.B: An identical board adds no new pressure for a bluff barrel.
explanations.C: If the opponent has folded, there is no one to barrel.
explanations.D: Correct. Scare cards that fit your story increase fold equity, making a second barrel effective.

questions.question: Continuing to barrel without fold equity or equity is generally:
options: A losing play, because you neither fold out better hands nor have outs to improve
Always profitable, since aggression always wins
Required whenever you bet the flop
A guaranteed way to win at showdown

explanations.A: Correct. Barreling with no fold equity and no draw simply burns chips.
explanations.B: Aggression without fold equity or equity is not automatically profitable.
explanations.C: There is no rule forcing continued barrels; giving up is often correct.
explanations.D: Barreling air does not improve your showdown chances.

questions.question: Barreling a turn that gives you a strong draw (a semi-bluff barrel) is attractive because:
options: You can never improve if you are called
You apply pressure now and can still win by completing your draw if called
It only works if you already have the nuts
It removes your opponent's ability to call

explanations.A: A semi-bluff barrel specifically has outs to improve when called.
explanations.B: Correct. The combination of fold equity and draw equity gives the barrel two ways to win.
explanations.C: Semi-bluff barrels hold draws, not the nuts.
explanations.D: Opponents can still call; the barrel relies on a mix of folds and draw equity.

questions.question: When the turn brings a third card of one suit on a two-tone flop, range equities shift because:
options: The board becomes drier with fewer draws
All possible draws are eliminated
Flush draws complete, so the made-flush combos in calling ranges increase
The hand must end immediately

explanations.A: Completing a flush makes the board wetter and more dangerous, not drier.
explanations.B: A flush card completes draws rather than eliminating possibilities.
explanations.C: Correct. The completing card turns prior draws into made flushes, changing who is now strong.
explanations.D: Play continues; the equity shift simply alters strategy.

questions.question: A 'scare card' on the turn is one that:
options: Never changes anyone's hand strength
Forces both players to check
Reduces the pot size automatically
Could plausibly improve hands and shifts perceived equity, prompting reevaluation

explanations.A: A true scare card precisely does change perceived or actual strength.
explanations.B: Scare cards influence strategy but do not force checks.
explanations.C: Cards do not alter pot size; only betting does.
explanations.D: Correct. Scare cards like overcards or flush-completers change the strength picture and bluffing dynamics.

questions.question: An overcard turn (e.g., an ace) after a small-pair-heavy flop tends to:
options: Favor the preflop raiser, who holds more aces and big cards
Favor the caller's small pairs every time
Have no effect on either range
Make the board paired

explanations.A: Correct. High-card turns generally help the raiser's range, supporting a continued barrel.
explanations.B: An overcard usually beats small pairs, helping the raiser, not the caller.
explanations.C: Overcards meaningfully shift equity toward the high-card-heavy range.
explanations.D: An overcard is a new rank; it does not pair the board unless it matches an existing card.

questions.question: When the turn pairs the bottom card of the board, a key effect is:
options: Flush draws are guaranteed to complete
More combos can now make trips or a full house, slightly favoring hands that contain that rank
The board becomes impossible to bluff
All straights become the nuts

explanations.A: A pairing card does not complete flush draws; it changes pair-based combos.
explanations.B: Correct. A pairing card adds full-house and trips potential, shifting equity toward hands holding that card.
explanations.C: Paired boards are often good bluffing textures, not impossible ones.
explanations.D: Pairing the board can actually devalue straights against full houses.

questions.question: A river card that completes an obvious draw should make you:
options: Always value bet bigger regardless
Ignore the board entirely
Reconsider value betting thinly and weigh whether your opponent just got there
Assume the opponent always missed

explanations.A: A completing river can beat your hand, so blindly sizing up risks getting raised or called by better.
explanations.B: Ignoring a draw-completing river leads to costly mistakes.
explanations.C: Correct. Completing rivers raise the chance villain improved, so thin value and bluff-catching must be reassessed.
explanations.D: Assuming villain missed on a completing river is an optimistic and dangerous read.

questions.question: Checking back the flop with a medium-strength hand in position is often done to:
options: Maximize the pot with a marginal hand
Guarantee folding out all worse hands
Reveal your hand to the opponent
Control the pot and realize showdown value cheaply

explanations.A: Bloating the pot with a marginal hand invites tough spots, not value.
explanations.B: Checking does not fold out worse hands; it simply passes the action.
explanations.C: Checking back conceals rather than reveals your holding.
explanations.D: Correct. Medium hands prefer to keep the pot small and reach a cheaper showdown.

questions.question: Pot control means:
options: Deliberately keeping the pot small with hands of marginal or medium strength
Always building the biggest pot possible
Folding every strong hand to stay safe
Going all-in on every street

explanations.A: Correct. Pot control limits risk and helps marginal hands reach showdown affordably.
explanations.B: That is the opposite of pot control.
explanations.C: Strong hands want value; pot control applies to marginal hands.
explanations.D: Maximal aggression is not pot control.

questions.question: A reason to check back rather than bet a strong-but-vulnerable hand can be to:
options: Make sure the opponent always folds
Induce bluffs from a weaker range that would fold to a bet
Avoid ever winning the pot
Force the dealer to deal a new flop

explanations.A: Checking is intended to keep weak hands in, not fold them out.
explanations.B: Correct. Checking can keep villain's bluffs and weak hands in, capturing value they would otherwise surrender.
explanations.C: Checking back is still aimed at winning; it just chooses a different line.
explanations.D: Checking does not change the community cards.

questions.question: Checking back the flop also benefits you by:
options: Making your range completely face-up
Removing the turn and river from play
Protecting your checking range so it is not always weak
Guaranteeing you the best hand

explanations.A: Mixing strong hands into checks conceals your range rather than exposing it.
explanations.B: Checking does not end the hand or skip streets.
explanations.C: Correct. Including some strong hands in the check-back range prevents opponents from attacking it freely.
explanations.D: Checking back controls the pot but never guarantees the winning hand.

questions.question: With a weak top pair out of position on a wet board, a frequent choice is to:
options: Bet large for value with no concern
Always shove all-in immediately
Fold every time without considering showdown
Check to control the pot, since betting bloats it against many better continues

explanations.A: A vulnerable one-pair hand betting large on a wet board often gets called by better and raised off equity.
explanations.B: Shoving a marginal hand over-commits and is rarely correct here.
explanations.C: A made top pair retains showdown value; folding immediately is too weak.
explanations.D: Correct. A vulnerable made hand OOP on a wet board prefers pot control to avoid difficult, large pots.

questions.question: A balanced river betting range mixes value and bluffs so that:
options: Opponents cannot profitably always-call or always-fold against you
You always have the nuts when you bet
You never get called by worse
You only ever bluff and never value bet

explanations.A: Correct. Proper balance makes the opponent indifferent, denying them an easy exploit.
explanations.B: Always having the nuts is unbalanced and lets opponents fold everything but premiums.
explanations.C: A balanced range explicitly wants calls from worse with value hands.
explanations.D: An all-bluff range is wildly unbalanced and easily exploited by calls.

questions.question: On the river, betting a pot-sized bet theoretically wants a value-to-bluff ratio around:
options: 1:1 value to bluffs for any sizing
2:1 value to bluffs, making the opponent indifferent to calling
All bluffs and no value
No bluffs at all under any sizing

explanations.A: The balanced ratio depends on sizing; pot-sized bets call for about 2:1, not 1:1.
explanations.B: Correct. For a pot-sized river bet, roughly two value combos per bluff keeps a bluff-catcher indifferent.
explanations.C: An all-bluff range is the opposite of balanced and is easily exploited.
explanations.D: A range with zero bluffs lets opponents fold all but the nuts profitably.

questions.question: Larger bet sizing on the river requires:
options: A higher proportion of bluffs than value
No relationship between size and ratio
A higher proportion of value relative to bluffs to stay balanced
Only value hands and never any bluffs

explanations.A: Larger bets call for more value, not more bluffs, to remain balanced.
explanations.B: Sizing directly determines the balanced value-to-bluff ratio.
explanations.C: Correct. Bigger bets risk more, so the value-to-bluff ratio must increase to keep opponents indifferent.
explanations.D: Even large bets retain some bluffs for balance; they are just outnumbered by value.

questions.question: When you bet the river with the nuts, you are:
options: Bluffing, since you want folds
Required to check the nuts
Trying to force a split pot
Value betting, aiming to be called by worse hands

explanations.A: You want calls, not folds, with the nuts; that is value, not a bluff.
explanations.B: There is no rule requiring you to check; value betting the nuts is standard.
explanations.C: The nuts wins outright; you are not aiming for a chop.
explanations.D: Correct. The nuts cannot be beaten, so betting it seeks calls from second-best holdings.

questions.question: A river bluff with a busted draw is most credible when:
options: The line and board let you represent the strong hands you are missing
Your line makes no sense for any strong hand
You hold the actual nuts
The pot contains no chips to win

explanations.A: Correct. A bluff works when your story is consistent with the value hands you could plausibly hold.
explanations.B: An incoherent story reduces fold equity and undermines the bluff.
explanations.C: Holding the nuts means betting for value, not bluffing with a busted draw.
explanations.D: A bluff needs a worthwhile pot; an empty pot makes it pointless.

questions.question: Facing a river bet with a bluff-catcher, your call is justified when:
options: You always hold the nuts
Your equity against villain's betting range exceeds the pot odds offered
The opponent never bluffs
Calling is free and risks nothing

explanations.A: A bluff-catcher is by definition not the nuts; the decision hinges on villain's bluff frequency.
explanations.B: Correct. A bluff-catcher calls profitably when villain bluffs often enough to beat the required pot odds.
explanations.C: If villain never bluffs, a bluff-catcher should fold, not call.
explanations.D: Calling a river bet always risks chips; pot odds govern the decision.

questions.question: On the river you can no longer:
options: Bet for value under any circumstance
Make any decision at all
Improve your hand with future cards, so decisions are made on current equity alone
Bluff because all bluffs are illegal

explanations.A: Value betting the river is common and correct with strong hands.
explanations.B: The river involves crucial bet, call, and fold decisions.
explanations.C: Correct. With no more cards to come, river play depends on showdown value and bluff dynamics, not draws.
explanations.D: River bluffs are a core, legal part of strategy.

questions.question: When deciding whether to value bet the river thinly, the key question is:
options: Can I fold out better hands by betting?
Will the dealer give me another card?
Does betting force a chop pot?
Will worse hands call often enough to make the bet profitable?

explanations.A: Folding out better hands is a bluff goal, not the value-bet question.
explanations.B: There are no more cards after the river; that is irrelevant.
explanations.C: Thin value seeks calls from worse, not a split pot.
explanations.D: Correct. Thin river value depends on whether the worse part of villain's range calls enough to beat the times you are behind.

questions.question: Checking back a marginal hand on the river instead of betting can be correct because:
options: Betting often only gets called by better and folds out worse, so showdown is more valuable
Marginal hands always have more value betting
Checking the river is against the rules
It guarantees the opponent folds

explanations.A: Correct. With a marginal hand, betting frequently isolates you against superior hands, so checking captures cheap showdown value.
explanations.B: Marginal hands often lose value by betting into a range that calls with better.
explanations.C: Checking the river is perfectly legal and frequently optimal.
explanations.D: Checking does not fold anyone out; it goes to showdown.

questions.question: Against a player who never bluffs the river, you should:
options: Call every river bet with any two cards
Fold your bluff-catchers, since their bets are almost always value
Always raise as a bluff
Assume they are always bluffing

explanations.A: Calling wide against a bluff-free range bleeds chips.
explanations.B: Correct. Without bluffs in villain's range, calling with a bluff-catcher is unprofitable.
explanations.C: Bluff-raising a player who only value bets is unlikely to fold them out.
explanations.D: Assuming bluffs against a no-bluff player is exactly the wrong read.

questions.question: The turn is often called a critical street because:
options: No betting is allowed on the turn
The turn ends the hand automatically
Bets are larger relative to the pot and set up the river decision
The turn card is always a blank

explanations.A: Betting is fully allowed on the turn; it is a key decision point.
explanations.B: The river still follows the turn; the hand is not over.
explanations.C: Correct. Turn play shapes pot size and commitment, heavily influencing river dynamics.
explanations.D: Turn cards frequently change equities; they are not always blanks.

questions.question: When you c-bet the flop and get called, then the turn is a blank, continuing to bet for value is best with:
options: Pure air with no equity and no fold equity
Hands you have already folded
The intention to chop the pot
Strong made hands that still want calls from worse and protection against draws

explanations.A: Barreling stone air on a blank turn with low fold equity is usually a losing play.
explanations.B: Folded hands cannot bet the turn.
explanations.C: Value betting seeks to win the pot, not chop it.
explanations.D: Correct. A blank turn lets strong hands keep value betting against the range that called the flop.

questions.question: Checking the turn back after c-betting the flop is reasonable when:
options: Your hand wants pot control or you lack equity to barrel profitably
You hold the absolute nuts and want max value
You are required to bet every turn
You want to inflate the pot with a weak hand

explanations.A: Correct. Checking back the turn controls the pot with medium hands and avoids spew with low-equity holdings.
explanations.B: The nuts usually keeps betting for value rather than checking back.
explanations.C: There is no rule forcing a turn bet; checking is a valid option.
explanations.D: Checking back keeps the pot small, the opposite of inflating it with a weak hand.

questions.question: A turn 'delayed' decision after the flop checks through often involves:
options: Assuming both players have the nuts
Reassessing ranges since both players showed some weakness on the flop
Ending the hand without a turn card
Ignoring all prior action

explanations.A: A checked-through flop suggests weakness, not that both hold the nuts.
explanations.B: Correct. A checked-through flop caps both ranges, so the turn requires fresh evaluation of who is now favored.
explanations.C: The turn card is dealt regardless; the hand continues.
explanations.D: Prior action informs the capped ranges and should not be ignored.

questions.question: Sizing up on the turn with a polarized range (strong value plus draws) is justified because:
options: It guarantees the opponent folds the best hand
Medium hands always need the biggest size
It charges draws and builds the pot to set up a large river bet
It is required after every flop c-bet

explanations.A: Larger sizing applies pressure but does not guarantee folding out the best hand.
explanations.B: Medium hands generally prefer smaller sizing or checks, not polarized overbets.
explanations.C: Correct. Larger turn bets with a polarized range maximize value and leverage for a big river barrel.
explanations.D: Turn sizing is a strategic choice, not a rule following a flop bet.

questions.question: In a multiway pot (three or more players), c-betting frequency should generally be:
options: Higher and more bluff-heavy than heads-up
Identical to heads-up play in every way
Always an all-in regardless of holding
Lower and more value-heavy, since more players means more chances someone connected

explanations.A: More opponents reduce fold equity, so bluffing should decrease, not increase, multiway.
explanations.B: Multiway dynamics differ significantly from heads-up; adjustments are required.
explanations.C: Blind shoving multiway ignores the increased likelihood of strong opposing hands.
explanations.D: Correct. With multiple opponents, fold equity drops and someone is more likely to hold a strong hand, so bluffing tightens.

questions.question: Bluffing tends to be less effective multiway because:
options: You must get every opponent to fold, and more players means more strong combos
Each extra player makes opponents fold more often
The pot is always smaller multiway
Bluffing is illegal with three players

explanations.A: Correct. Fold equity compounds against multiple players, so a bluff faces longer odds of success.
explanations.B: More players make a successful bluff less likely, not more.
explanations.C: Multiway pots are usually larger, not smaller.
explanations.D: Bluffing remains legal multiway; it is simply less profitable.

questions.question: Value betting thresholds in multiway pots typically:
options: Loosen, since more players means weaker ranges
Tighten, because the chance someone holds a strong hand rises with more players
Stay exactly the same as heads-up
Disappear because value betting is impossible

explanations.A: Additional players raise the chance of a strong hand, so value standards tighten.
explanations.B: Correct. You need a stronger hand to value bet confidently into multiple opponents.
explanations.C: Multiway value betting requires stronger hands than heads-up.
explanations.D: Value betting is still possible multiway; the bar is just higher.

questions.question: Protection betting a vulnerable made hand multiway is often:
options: Unnecessary, since multiway draws never complete
Only correct heads-up and never multiway
More important, because more opponents mean more live draws to charge
A reason to check every made hand

explanations.A: Multiway draws complete frequently; protection is more, not less, important.
explanations.B: Protection is especially relevant multiway due to more live draws.
explanations.C: Correct. With several players holding potential draws, charging them with a bet protects your made hand's equity.
explanations.D: Vulnerable made hands often want to bet for protection multiway, not check.

questions.question: Acting in position (last to act postflop) is advantageous because:
options: You are forced to act first every street
You must put in more chips than others
You always hold the best hand
You see opponents' actions before deciding, gaining information each street

explanations.A: In position means acting last, not first.
explanations.B: Position does not require contributing more chips.
explanations.C: Position improves decisions but never guarantees the best hand.
explanations.D: Correct. Last action provides information and lets you control the pot and bluff or value bet more precisely.

questions.question: Out-of-position play is harder primarily because:
options: You must act first without knowing the opponent's intended action
You always have a stronger range
You see the opponent's cards in advance
You are guaranteed to win more pots

explanations.A: Correct. Acting first cedes information, complicating value betting, bluffing, and pot control.
explanations.B: Being OOP does not confer a stronger range; it is an informational disadvantage.
explanations.C: OOP players do not see hole cards; they act with less information.
explanations.D: OOP is a disadvantage, not a guarantee of winning more pots.

questions.question: A key in-position tool unavailable out of position is:
options: Forcing the opponent to act after you
Checking behind to take a free card or a cheap showdown
Seeing the river before the turn
Posting a smaller blind

explanations.A: In position, the opponent acts before you; you cannot force them to act after.
explanations.B: Correct. The in-position player can close the action with a check, controlling the pot precisely.
explanations.C: Cards are dealt in order regardless of position.
explanations.D: Position is not about blind sizes.

questions.question: Because position improves equity realization, you can profitably:
options: Play only the very best hands in late position
Play any two cards from under the gun profitably
Open and continue with a wider range in late position than in early position
Ignore your hand strength entirely

explanations.A: Position lets you widen your range, not restrict it to premiums.
explanations.B: Early position with poor realization demands a tighter, not wider, range.
explanations.C: Correct. Better realization in position justifies playing more hands than from early seats.
explanations.D: Hand strength still matters; position adjusts ranges, not eliminates the need for strength.

questions.question: An overbet is a bet that:
options: Is always smaller than half the pot
Equals exactly the size of the pot
Is illegal in no-limit games
Exceeds the size of the current pot

explanations.A: That is a small bet; overbets are larger than the whole pot.
explanations.B: A bet equal to the pot is pot-sized, not an overbet.
explanations.C: Overbets are perfectly legal in no-limit; you may bet up to your stack.
explanations.D: Correct. Overbets are larger than the pot and are used with very polarized, nut-advantaged ranges.

questions.question: Overbetting is most effective when you have:
options: A nut advantage and a polarized range against a capped opponent
A merged range of only medium hands
The weakest range at the table
No strong hands in your range at all

explanations.A: Correct. Overbets leverage strong nutted hands and bluffs against a range that cannot comfortably call big.
explanations.B: Merged medium ranges prefer smaller sizing; they lack the nut hands overbets exploit.
explanations.C: Overbetting from a weak, capped range is exploitable and unwise.
explanations.D: Overbets need value hands at the top to be balanced and credible.

questions.question: Overbetting the river puts maximum pressure on a capped range because:
options: The opponent always has the nuts to call
The opponent lacks hands strong enough to call comfortably, so folds increase
Overbets give the opponent great odds
It forces a chop pot

explanations.A: A capped range by definition lacks the nutted hands needed to call an overbet.
explanations.B: Correct. A capped range cannot profitably call large sizing, so an overbet generates more folds and folds out bluff-catchers.
explanations.C: Overbets give worse odds to call, not better ones.
explanations.D: Overbets aim to win the whole pot through folds or value, not to chop.

questions.question: A balanced overbetting range still includes bluffs because:
options: Bluffs make the overbet a value bet
Bluffs are required by no-limit rules
Without bluffs, opponents could fold everything but the nuts profitably
Bluffs guarantee the pot is chopped

explanations.A: Bluffs do not convert to value; they balance the value hands.
explanations.B: Including bluffs is strategic balance, not a rule.
explanations.C: Correct. Including bluffs keeps the overbet credible and the opponent indifferent.
explanations.D: Bluffs aim to win via folds, not to force a chop.

questions.question: Your range is 'capped' when:
options: Your range contains every nutted hand
You hold only the single best hand
You have folded all your hands
Your line excludes the strongest possible hands, leaving a ceiling on your strength

explanations.A: Holding all the nutted hands is uncapped, the opposite of capped.
explanations.B: One nut hand is not a capped range; capped means lacking the top hands.
explanations.C: A capped range still contains live hands; it just lacks the strongest ones.
explanations.D: Correct. A capped range has a maximum strength below the nuts, exposing it to large bets.

questions.question: Checking back the flop frequently can cap your range because:
options: You signal you likely lack the strongest hands that would have bet
It proves you hold the nuts
It removes your opponent's range
It guarantees you win the pot

explanations.A: Correct. Always checking strong hands elsewhere makes your check-back range readably weaker.
explanations.B: Checking back typically suggests the absence of the strongest hands, not their presence.
explanations.C: Your checking has no effect on the opponent's range composition.
